title: Media Analytics
subtitle: Viewer experience data for streaming operations

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: Media Analytics is the Nielsen ratings for the streaming age — but instead of sampling a few households, you measure every single viewer, every second, across every device and ISP.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Akamai Media Analytics provides real-time and historical data on streaming quality:

- **Viewer Experience**: Rebuffering ratio, startup time, bitrate distribution, error rates
- **Audience Metrics**: Concurrent viewers, geographic distribution, device/OS/browser breakdown
- **Engagement**: Play duration, drop-off points, ad completion rates
- **QoS Alerts**: Real-time thresholds on rebuffering, errors, and viewer drops

Data sources: CDN logs (DataStream), player-side beacons (mPulse/MediaMelon), and AMD-level metrics. Dashboards available in Akamai Control Center.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: During a live event, the operations team monitors concurrent viewers, rebuffering by ISP, and error spikes by region. If rebuffering spikes in a specific ASN, they can escalate to the ISP or reroute through alternate PoPs. Post-event, the data feeds capacity planning for the next broadcast.
"Media Analytics = every viewer, every second. Real-time QoS monitoring + historical analysis for capacity planning."

quiz.question: During a live broadcast, rebuffering spikes for viewers on a specific ISP. What do you check first?

options.explain: If it's ISP-specific, the issue is downstream of the origin.

options.text: Media Analytics ISP/ASN breakdown — identify the affected network and check PoP health in that region
options.explain: ISP-specific issues are usually last-mile or peering problems. Analytics pinpoints the ASN; ops can reroute or escalate.
Player issues would affect all ISPs, not just one.
Encoding issues would affect all viewers regardless of ISP.

quiz.question: What's the difference between Media Analytics and mPulse?

options.explain: Different products with different focus areas.

options.text: Media Analytics focuses on streaming-specific metrics (rebuffering, concurrent viewers, bitrate); mPulse focuses on web page performance (Core Web Vitals, page load)
options.explain: Media Analytics is video-native. mPulse is web-native. Both are RUM, but for different content types.
Both measure real users. They measure different things.

options.text: Media Analytics is only for VOD
options.explain: It covers live, VOD, and download delivery.
